beats.narration_text: Guten Tag — this is Liam, in for Bear. Ask Claude to write a technical spec for a new API. Most of the time, Claude writes for you, not for your readers. The doc-coauthoring skill changes that. It runs a three-stage workflow: close the knowledge gap first, build section by section second, and test with a reader who wasn't in the room. Let me show you how it works.

props.command: Write a technical spec for our new payments API. It needs to go to the engineering team and product.
output: Stage 1: Context Gathering — 5 meta questions + info dump
Stage 2: Refinement — brainstorm → curate → draft → refine per section
Stage 3: Reader Testing — fresh Claude, no context bleed

beats.act: anatomy — three stages + trigger + doc types
beats.narration_text: The doc-coauthoring skill is a structured workflow for collaborative document creation. The trigger fires when the user mentions writing documentation, proposals, technical specs, or decision docs — PRDs, design docs, RFCs, any substantial writing task. The workflow has three stages: context gathering, refinement and structure, and reader testing. Stage three is the key — it tests the document with a fresh Claude that has no context from this conversation.

Stage one: context gathering. The goal is to close the gap between what the user knows and what Claude knows. Claude starts with five meta-context questions — what type of document, who is the audience, what is the desired impact, is there a template, and any constraints. Then Claude asks the user to dump all context: background, team discussions, why alternative solutions aren't being used, org dynamics, timeline pressures. The exit condition is when Claude can ask about edge cases and trade-offs without needing basics explained.

props.sparkLine: Dump context. Claude closes the gap.

beats.narration_text: Stage two: refinement and structure. For each section, Claude runs a six-step loop. Clarify: five to ten questions. Brainstorm: five to twenty options — more for complex sections. Curate: user keeps, removes, or combines. Gap check: what is still missing. Draft: str-replace fills the placeholder. Refine: iterate until no substantial changes. The quality gate triggers after three iterations with no changes — ask if anything can be removed without losing information. At eighty percent completion, re-read the whole document and cut slop.

Stage three: reader testing. This is the most underrated step. The skill invokes a fresh Claude with no context from this conversation — that is the reader. In Claude Code, this is automatic: Claude predicts reader questions, runs them through a sub-agent, and summarizes what the reader got wrong. In claude.ai, it is manual: open a new conversation, paste the doc, and run the questions yourself. The exit condition is when reader Claude answers consistently correctly and stops surfacing new gaps.

props.sparkLine: A fresh Claude finds your blind spots.

beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown moment. The insight behind this skill is that context bleeding is real. After a thirty-minute info dump, what makes sense to you looks like jargon to a fresh reader. What the skill gets right: the brainstorm and curate loop prevents the blank-page problem. Reader testing catches blind spots structurally. What it costs: this is a long workflow — it is designed for documents that will actually be reviewed at scale, not a quick write-up. The str-replace discipline breaks down if the user edits the doc directly and doesn't say so.

props.sparkLine: Write for the reader. Test with the reader.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. The doc-coauthoring skill is a three-stage workflow that treats the document as something that must work without the author in the room. Stage one closes the knowledge gap. Stage two builds section by section with a quality gate. Stage three tests with a fresh Claude — no shared context, no shared assumptions, no shared vocabulary.

artifactLines: Stage 1: info dump + 5 meta questions → exit when edge cases can be discussed
Stage 2: 6-step per-section loop — brainstorm → curate → draft → refine
Quality gate: 3 iterations with no change → ask what can be cut
Stage 3: fresh Claude test — no context from this conversation
Best for: docs that will be reviewed at scale (PRD, RFC, decision doc)
Limit: str_replace discipline breaks if user edits doc directly

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open a Claude session. Paste this: I need to write a decision doc about whether to migrate our auth service to OAuth 2.0. Walk me through the doc-coauthoring workflow. Then stop and watch what Claude does before it writes anything. Does it ask the five meta-context questions? Does it wait for your info dump before starting to draft? That is the gate.

props.command: I need to write a decision doc about whether to migrate our auth service to OAuth 2.0. Walk me through the doc-coauthoring workflow.
props.runningText: Watch: does Claude gather context before drafting? That is the gate.
output: Expected: Stage 1 — 5 meta questions before any drafting
Expected: info dump requested before clarifying questions
Stage 3 test: open a fresh Claude, paste the doc, ask reader questions

beats.narration_text: Doc Co-Authoring. Liam, in for Bear.
